g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive guide on changing file permissions in Linux and Unix systems.
File permissions are the foundation of Linux security, controlling who can access, modify, or execute files and directories.
g.text: Every file in Linux has permissions determining who can read, write, or execute it. This is
essential for securing sensitive data, configuring web servers, and managing multi-user systems.

g.data-narration: In this lesson, you will learn permission types, categories, chmod commands, and special permissions with practical examples.
Linux has three permission types: read (r), write (w), and execute (x). Their meaning differs between files and directories.
g.text: r = view content | w = modify/delete | x = run program
r = list files | w = create/delete | x = traverse (enter)

g.data-narration: Permissions apply to three user categories: owner (u), group (g), and others (o). Each gets independent permission settings.
The ls -l command shows a 10-character permission string. The first character indicates file type, followed by three sets of rwx permissions.
The first character of ls -l output shows file type: dash for regular files, d for directories, l for symbolic links, and more.
Permissions use octal numbers: read equals 4, write equals 2, execute equals 1. Add them together for each category.
g.text: Each permission has a value. Add them to get the octal number for each category:

g.data-narration: Common permission patterns: 755 for executables, 644 for regular files, 600 for private files, and 777 for world-writable (risky!).
g.text: WARNING: Never use 777 on production systems - it is a major security vulnerability!

g.data-narration: Symbolic mode uses letters to add, remove, or set permissions. Use u for owner, g for group, o for others, and a for all.
g.text: Use operators (+ adds, - removes, = sets exactly) with user categories:

g.data-narration: The chmod command changes permissions in both numeric and symbolic modes. Here are practical examples for common scenarios.
Linux has three special permissions: SUID (4), SGID (2), and Sticky Bit (1). They appear as s or t in the execute position.
umask determines default permissions for new files and directories. Files get 666, directories get 777, minus the umask value.
Example: Setting up a web directory with 755 for directories and 644 for files - owner has full access, others can only read.
g.text: Configure a web app readable by server but writable only by owner:

g.data-narration: Example: Creating a shared group directory using SGID so all files inherit the group ownership automatically.
g.text: Create a directory where multiple users in the developers group can collaborate:
The s means SGID is set - new files inherit the group!

g.data-narration: Example: Securing a private SSH key with 600 permissions - only the owner can read it, preventing unauthorized access.
Key formulas to remember: octal values (r=4,w=2,x=1), common patterns (755,644,600), and special permissions (SUID=4,SGID=2,Sticky=1).
